highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.121 Released
highlights.summary: This release adds an `alwaysLoad` option to MCP server configs so specific tools are always available without being deferred by tool-search. It also introduces `claude plugin prune` to clean up orphaned plugin dependencies, keeping your environment tidy. Beginners will appreciate the new type-to-filter feature for faster navigation.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.119 Released
highlights.summary: Settings configured via `/config` (such as theme, editor mode, and verbosity) now persist to `~/.claude/settings.json` and respect the full project/local/policy override hierarchy, so your preferences survive restarts. A new `prUrlTemplate` setting lets teams point the PR badge footer at custom code-review URLs. This makes Claude Code much more consistent for team and enterprise setups.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.118 Released
highlights.summary: Vim users get a major upgrade with visual mode (`v`) and visual-line mode (`V`) support, including selection operators and visual feedback. The `/cost` and `/stats` commands have been merged into `/usage` for a cleaner experience. Custom themes can now be created and switched directly from `/theme`, making it easier to personalise your workspace.

highlights.title: Introducing Claude Opus 4.7
highlights.summary: Anthropic has announced Claude Opus 4.7, the latest addition to the Opus model family. This new model brings enhanced capabilities relevant to complex coding and reasoning tasks, making it a compelling option for developers pushing Claude Code on harder problems. Check the Anthropic blog for full details on benchmarks and availability.

highlights.title: Claude Code Desktop App: Beginner's Guide (No Terminal Needed)
highlights.summary: This YouTube tutorial walks complete beginners through using Claude Code via the desktop app, so you don't need to touch a terminal to get started. It covers the core interface, how to open projects, and how to issue your first coding instructions. A great starting point if the command line feels intimidating.

highlights.title: An update on recent Claude Code quality reports
highlights.summary: Anthropic published an engineering postmortem addressing quality issues that users reported with Claude Code around April 23rd. Reading it gives developers insight into what went wrong, how Anthropic diagnosed the problem, and what safeguards are being added. Understanding these incidents helps you set better expectations and know when to escalate unexpected AI behaviour.

highlights.title: Ask HN: How are you using AI code assistants on large messy legacy code bases?
highlights.summary: This Hacker News thread collects real-world strategies from experienced developers (including 20+ year veterans) on applying AI coding tools to sprawling, poorly-documented legacy codebases. The discussion surfaces practical limitations and creative workarounds that go beyond toy examples. Essential reading if you're trying to bring Claude Code into production on inherited code.

highlights.title: EvanFlow – A TDD-driven feedback loop for Claude Code
highlights.summary: EvanFlow is an open-source tool that wraps Claude Code in a test-driven development feedback loop, automatically running tests and feeding results back to Claude until the code passes. This reduces the need for constant manual supervision and helps enforce code quality from the start. Developers comfortable with TDD will find it a natural fit for agentic coding workflows.

tips_of_the_day: Use `alwaysLoad: true` in your MCP server config for tools you rely on constantly — this prevents Claude Code from deferring them during tool-search and keeps your most-used integrations always ready to go.
Run `claude plugin prune` periodically to remove orphaned auto-installed plugin dependencies; a leaner plugin environment means faster startup times and fewer unexpected conflicts.

When starting with AI on a legacy codebase, give Claude Code a single well-bounded module rather than the whole repo — providing focused context with a clear task (e.g. 'add tests to this file') produces far better results than broad open-ended requests.
